question: GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks): how do generator and discriminator interact?

options: Two networks play minimax game: generator $G(z)$ maps noise to samples; discriminator $D(x)$ classifies real vs fake. Loss: $\min_G\max_D \mathbb E_{x\sim p_{\rm data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb E_{z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$. At equilibrium $p_G = p_{\rm data}$. Challenges: mode collapse, training instability — addressed by WGAN, spectral norm, R1 regularisation, StyleGAN architecture

GANs use single network with no adversary (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)

Generator trained on labelled data only (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (not supported by published research or production deployments)

Discriminator is the only learnable component (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory)
passive.explanation: GAN (Goodfellow 2014): implicit generative modelling. Generator $G$: learns mapping $z \sim \mathcal N(0, I) \to x$; no explicit density. Discriminator $D$: outputs probability $x$ is real. Optimal $D$ for fixed $G$: $D^* = p_{\rm data}/(p_{\rm data} + p_G)$; substituting gives Jensen-Shannon divergence minimisation for $G$. Training: alternating gradient steps on $D$ and $G$. Issues: mode collapse (G covers subset of data modes), vanishing gradients when $D$ too strong, non-convergence cycles. Improvements: WGAN (Arjovsky 2017) — Wasserstein distance, more stable; spectral norm (Miyato 2018); progressive growing (Karras 2017); StyleGAN (Karras 2018-21) — disentangled latent, $\sim$photorealistic faces. Apps: image generation, super-resolution (SRGAN), domain transfer (CycleGAN), inpainting. Mostly superseded by diffusion for state-of-art image generation but still used for fast inference.

active.prompt: Match each generative model family with its key property:

pairs.definition: Adversarial training; implicit likelihood; sharp samples but unstable training; StyleGAN, BigGAN
Encoder-decoder + KL prior; explicit likelihood (ELBO); blurry samples but stable; useful for representations
Noise-to-data reverse process; SOTA quality; slow inference but improving; Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, Sora
Factorise $p(x) = \prod p(x_i|x_{<i})$; tractable likelihood; fast inference per token, slow for sequences

active.explanation: Generative model trade-offs: explicit vs implicit likelihood; sample quality vs speed vs diversity; training stability. Recent SOTA: diffusion for images/video/audio; autoregressive for text; hybrid (DiT, transformer + diffusion). Multi-modal: GPT-4o, Gemini, Sora combine multiple modalities.

question: Variational Autoencoder (VAE): explain encoder, decoder, and ELBO.

options: Encoder $q_\phi(z|x)$ outputs Gaussian over latent $z$; decoder $p_\theta(x|z)$ outputs reconstruction; prior $p(z) = \mathcal N(0, I)$. ELBO: $\log p(x) \ge \mathbb E_{q_\phi}[\log p_\theta(x|z)] - \text{KL}(q_\phi(z|x)\|p(z))$. Reparameterise $z = \mu + \sigma\epsilon$ for gradient through sampling. Decoder produces continuous samples; KL term regularises latent

VAE has no encoder (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture)

Loss is identical to GAN minimax (not supported by published research or production deployments) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)

Latent space is discrete categorical (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory)
passive.explanation: VAE (Kingma-Welling 2013): variational inference + neural networks. Goal: maximise log-likelihood of data $\log p(x)$, but $p(x) = \int p_\theta(x|z)p(z)dz$ intractable. Lower bound (ELBO): $\log p(x) \ge \mathcal L(\phi, \theta) = \mathbb E_{z\sim q_\phi(z|x)}[\log p_\theta(x|z)] - \text{KL}(q_\phi(z|x) \| p(z))$. Reconstruction term + KL regulariser pushing $q_\phi$ to prior. Reparameterisation trick: $z = \mu_\phi(x) + \sigma_\phi(x)\odot\epsilon$, $\epsilon\sim\mathcal N(0,I)$ — differentiable through sampling. Issues: posterior collapse (KL=0, decoder ignores $z$); blurry samples (mean of Gaussian decoder). Improvements: $\beta$-VAE (Higgins 2017, disentanglement), VQ-VAE (van den Oord 2017, discrete codebook — basis for DALL-E 1, recent video models), NVAE, $\sigma$-VAE. Foundational for representation learning, used in modern diffusion as latent compression (Stable Diffusion latent space).

question: Diffusion models: forward and reverse process, and the training objective.

options: Forward: gradually add Gaussian noise over $T$ steps, $q(x_t|x_{t-1}) = \mathcal N(x_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}x_{t-1}, \beta_t I)$; final $x_T \approx \mathcal N(0, I)$. Reverse: learn $p_\theta(x_{t-1}|x_t)$ to denoise. Training: predict noise $\epsilon$ from $x_t$ at random $t$; loss $\|\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)\|^2$. Sampling: iterative denoising from pure noise

Diffusion adds noise once, doesn't iterate (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture)
Reverse process not learned; just adds noise (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies)

Training loss is cross-entropy (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (not supported by published research or production deployments) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop))
passive.explanation: DDPM (Ho 2020): forward Markov chain $q(x_{1:T}|x_0)$ progressively corrupts data with Gaussian noise. Closed-form: $x_t = \sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\epsilon$ with $\bar\alpha_t = \prod(1-\beta_s)$. Reverse: learn denoiser $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)$. Training: sample $t$, $\epsilon$, form $x_t$, minimise $\|\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta\|^2$ — much simpler than VAE's ELBO. Equivalent to score matching: $\epsilon_\theta \propto -\nabla_x\log p_t(x)$ score function. Sampling: $T = 1000$ original DDPM; DDIM (Song 2020) accelerates to $\sim$50 steps; classifier-free guidance (Ho 2022) for conditional generation; latent diffusion (Rombach 2022, Stable Diffusion) operates in VAE latent space → 8× faster. Architectures: U-Net (image), DiT (Diffusion Transformer, Sora). Apps: text-to-image (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, Midjourney), text-to-video (Sora, Veo), audio (AudioLDM), 3D (DreamFusion), molecular design.

question: Classifier-free guidance: how does it enable controllable diffusion generation?

options: Train single network on both conditional and unconditional (drop condition with prob 10\%); at inference, extrapolate: $\hat\epsilon = \epsilon_\theta(x, c) + w[\epsilon_\theta(x, c) - \epsilon_\theta(x, \emptyset)]$ with guidance scale $w > 0$. Higher $w$: stronger conditioning, less diverse. Standard $w = 7.5$ in Stable Diffusion. Trade-off: quality and adherence vs diversity

Need separate classifier network always (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (not supported by published research or production deployments)

Cannot condition diffusion models (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses)

Conditioning requires retraining for each prompt (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture)
passive.explanation: Classifier guidance (Dhariwal 2021): use trained classifier $p(c|x)$ to compute $\nabla\log p(c|x)$ added to score during sampling — boosts class-conditional generation but requires separate classifier. Classifier-free guidance (Ho 2022): elegant — train conditional and unconditional in single model (dropout the condition during training). At sampling: $\hat\epsilon_\theta = (1+w)\epsilon_\theta(x_t, c) - w\epsilon_\theta(x_t, \emptyset)$ — extrapolates in direction of conditional-vs-unconditional difference. $w = 0$: vanilla conditional; large $w$: very high adherence but artefacts. Used everywhere: Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, Imagen. Text-to-image: $c$ = text embedding (T5 or CLIP encoder). Image conditioning: ControlNet (Zhang 2023) — injects edge maps, depth, pose. Multi-step refinement: ControlNet + IP-Adapter + LoRA composability.

question: Latent diffusion (Stable Diffusion): why operate in latent space?

options: VAE encodes 512×512 image to 64×64 latent (8× compression); diffusion in latent space — same model capacity, 8² = 64× compute reduction. Stable Diffusion architecture: pretrained VAE + U-Net latent denoiser + CLIP text encoder. Trained on LAION-5B; open-source; spawned ecosystem (Midjourney inspired, Adobe Firefly, etc.)
Latent space is meaningless; pixel diffusion better (not supported by published research or production deployments) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds)
Stable Diffusion uses GAN, not diffusion (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)
Latent diffusion is slower than pixel (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory)
passive.explanation: Pixel-space diffusion expensive: GLIDE, DALL-E 2 use full-resolution U-Net at 64-256 spatial; cascaded upsampling. Latent Diffusion (Rombach et al.\ 2022 → Stable Diffusion 1.4 Aug 2022): encode 512² to 4×64² latent via pretrained VAE (KL-divergence regularised), diffuse there, decode. Massive efficiency win: comparable quality at $\sim 50\times$ less compute. Architecture: U-Net with cross-attention on CLIP text embeddings; trained 200k GPU-hours total. Open weights led to ecosystem: LoRA fine-tuning, ControlNet, IP-Adapter, image-to-image, inpainting, AnimateDiff. SD 2.x, SDXL (2023): larger, higher resolution. SD3, Flux.1, Imagen 3 (2024): use Diffusion Transformer (DiT) instead of U-Net — better scaling. Sora (OpenAI 2024): DiT in latent space for video, key open-source variant: Mochi.

question: Flow matching and rectified flow: alternative to diffusion?

options: Frame generative modelling as learning vector field transforming prior to data distribution; train via flow matching loss without time-discretisation. Rectified flow (Liu 2022): straightens trajectories iteratively — fewer integration steps, ideally 1-step generation. Stable Diffusion 3, Flux.1 use flow matching; competitive quality with diffusion at faster sampling

Flow matching is exactly diffusion (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)

Slower than diffusion always (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (not supported by published research or production deployments)

Cannot generate images (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic)
passive.explanation: Conditional flow matching (Lipman 2022, Liu 2022): unified framework subsuming diffusion + continuous normalising flows. Define probability path $p_t(x)$ from prior to data; train network to predict velocity field $u_\theta(x, t)$ matching target — simple regression. No SDE simulation in training. Rectified flow: starts from straight-line interpolation $x_t = (1-t)x_0 + t x_1$; trains velocity, then 'reflows' for straighter paths. Goal: fewer integration steps (NFEs = number of function evaluations). Diffusion typically 25-50 NFEs; rectified flow can do 1-4. Models: Stable Diffusion 3 (SD3, 2024): MM-DiT with flow matching; Flux.1 (Black Forest Labs 2024) — strong open-weight model; Lumiere, Veo also flow-based. Theoretical equivalence: VP-SDE (variance-preserving diffusion) is flow matching in disguise. Practical advantages: simpler training, faster sampling, flexible boundary conditions.
